It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Correlates of rigorous and credible transnational governance: A cross-sectoral analysis of best practice compliance in eco-labeling Hamish van der Ven Department of Political Science, University of Toronto, Toronto, ON, Canada Abstract The number of eco-labeling schemes is rising dramatically, yet the rigor and credibility of such schemes remains uneven.Whereas some eco-labeling organizations (ELOs) comply with best practice guidelines designed to increase the credibility of their standards through attention to good operating principles, such as transparency and impartiality, others do not. Within this article, I attempt to explain this variation through multivariate regression analysis of an original cross-sectoral dataset of transnational ELO policies and practices. I ﬁnd compelling evidence to suggest that ELOs with environmental non-governmental organization (ENGO) partners, nonproﬁt structures, or broad transnational reach are most likely to comply with best practices. I also ﬁnd that private ELOs are more likely to disregard best practices than public ones. Conversely, I ﬁnd little evidence that levels of industry funding or sector-speciﬁc competition dynamics affect best practice compliance. This study contributes new data, a new method of comparison, and new ﬁndings to the growing literature on transnational governance. Keywords: best practice, eco-labeling, private authority, regulatory rigor, rule-setting transnational governance. 1. Introduction Over the past 35 years, the number of organizations engaged in environmental certiﬁcation and labeling (eco- labeling) has risen dramatically. Eco-labeling organizations (ELOs), such as the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC) and the Marine Stewardship Council (MSC) set rules about proper environmental conduct intended to guide and constrain companies on a voluntary basis. While states and international organizations continue to create and operate eco-labels, increasingly, the most widely used eco-labeling standards are created and maintained by civil society groups, industry associations, corporations, hybrid public–private organizations, and purpose-built for- proﬁt or not-for-proﬁt organizations. Perhaps unsurprisingly, the shift of rulemaking authority away from states and multilateral institutions and towards non-state actors has raised legitimate questions about the rigor and credibility of eco-labeling systems (Bowles 2011, p. 1). Many of these concerns center around the political motivations of private rulemaking bodies (Cutler et al. 1999, p. 369; Büthe & Mattli 2013, pp. 11–12), the negative effects of competition between different eco-labeling systems (Abbott & Snidal 2010, p. 324; Fransen 2011; Cashore & Stone 2014), and the lack of accountability in certain private governance schemes (Van Harten 2005, p. 615; Fuchs & Kalfagianni 2010, p. 10). In response to these concerns, a number of meta-standards or best practice guidelines have emerged with the goal of increasing the rigor and credibility of eco-labeling by encouraging attention to good operating principles, such as transparency, impartiality, and inclusiveness. In this paper, I ask under what conditions ELOs will be most likely to comply with globally-recognized best practice guidelines for eco-labeling. I answer this question by testing a range of theories using multivariate regression analysis of an original dataset of ELO policies and practices in 2013. This unique dataset brings together, for the ﬁrst time, information on 123 transnational ELOs comprising the full known population of transnational eco-labeling organizations at the time the study was conducted. I use this original dataset to probe hypotheses on the conditions for compliance with best practice in eco-labeling. I argue that best practice compliance is a useful proxy for the overall rigor and credibility Correspondence: Hamish van der Ven, Department of Political Science, University of Toronto, Sidney Smith Hall, Room 3018, 100 St. George Street, Toronto, ON, Canada M5S 3G3. Moreover, a focus on best practice compliance has the added value of allowing researchers to compare ELOs across sectors and environmental issue areas. My ﬁndings yield several surprising results that challenge past scholarship on eco-labeling and private gover- nance. I ﬁnd no evidence to suggest that industry-funded ELOs are less compliant with best practices than non- industry-funded ELOs. Nor do I ﬁnd evidence that intra-sector competition between ELOs leads to heightened or diminished attention to best practices. I do, however, ﬁnd evidence to suggest that, on balance, private ELOs are more likely to disregard best practices than public ones, and that ELOs developed in partnership with existing environ- mental groups, structured as nonproﬁts, or possessing broad transnational reach are more likely to comply with best practices than ELOs without these attributes. These ﬁndings suggest that a simple public/private dichotomy offers limited explanatory leverage for theorizing credible transnational governance. Attention to procedural rigor and credibility depend on the characteristics of the private organizations involved. This study contributes to the burgeoning literature on eco-labeling and rule-setting transnational governance by establishing a unique method of comparing eco-labeling organizations across sectors. It also contributes to the ﬁeld by offering an original large N cross-sectoral database through which to probe the plausibility of existing theories on the conditions for rigorous and credible transnational governance. I organize the paper as follows: ﬁrst, I concep- tualize eco-labeling as a form of rule-setting transnational governance. Next, I describe best practice in eco-labeling, explain its importance, and outline my dependent variable. I then review previous theories on regulatory rigor in transnational governance and derive a series of testable hypotheses. Following this, I explain my research methods, describe my data, and review my ﬁndings. I conclude by discussing the implications of my ﬁndings and suggesting avenues for future research. 2. Eco-labeling as rule-setting transnational governance In this article, I conceptualize eco-labeling as a form of rule-setting transnational governance.1 Transnational governance occurs “when networks operating in the transnational sphere authoritatively steer constituents towards public goals” (Andonova et al. 2009, p. 56). Rule-setting transnational governance mirrors traditional systems of intergovernmental or domestic governance inasmuch as a central authority establishes rules intended to guide and constrain the constituents of a governance network (Andonova et al. 2009, p. 65). In this case, ELOs establish rules about proper environmental conduct intended to guide and constrain companies that voluntarily agree to be bound by these rules. For example, the FSC establishes rules to ensure that FSC-certiﬁed companies harvest timber in a manner that maintains the forest’s biodiversity, productivity, and ecological processes, among other factors (Forest Stewardship Council 2015). Rule-setting transnational governance arrangements are increasingly common. Indeed, we see them used in a number of areas, including accounting standards, fair trade labels, and transnational carbon markets. Consequently, a focus on eco-labels is a useful way of investigating a broader and increasingly salient class of transnational governance initiatives. Past studies on this topic have mainly consisted of small n case studies that use process-tracing within a single industrial sector or a handful of sectors; for example, accounting (Camfferman & Zeff 2007), apparel and forestry (Bartley 2003, 2005, 2007), electro-technical (Büthe 2010a), ﬁnancial reporting (Büthe & Mattli 2013), ﬁsheries (Gulbrandsen 2010, 2014), forestry (Cashore 2002; Cashore et al. 2004; Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Auld et al. 2008; Overdevest 2010; Overdevest & Zeitlin 2014), labor (Fransen 2011; Fransen & Burgoon 2012), and sanitary paper product standards (Harrison 1999).2 While these papers have yielded many valuable insights, few of their hypotheses have been submitted to a broader test for generalizability. This paper answers a call for more generalized testing of existing hypotheses that have been developed within particular commercial sectors (Gulbrandsen 2014, p. 89). The original dataset of 123 transnational ELOs used in this study provides an empirical base of unprecedented scope through which to test the generalizability of existing theories in a cross-national and cross-sectoral context. This paper also asks a different question than previous research on rule-setting transnational governance. Past studies have attempted to explain the rise of transnational governance (Cutler et al. 1999; Hall & Biersteker 2002; Bartley 2003, 2007; Green 2013a); whether it can be legitimate (Cashore 2002; Cashore et al. 2004; Borzel & Risse 2005; Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Black 2008); how states interact with non-state governance schemes (Abbott & Best practice in eco-labeling H. van der Ven © 2015 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 277 Page 3: Snidal 2010; Bartley 2011; Eberlein et al. 2014; Gulbrandsen 2014; Hale & Roger 2014); why ﬁrms join particular governance schemes (Potoski & Prakash 2005; Darnall et al. 2010); why certain governance schemes seek stakeholder input more than others (Carmin et al. 2003); why some sectors have more fragmented governance systems than others (Fransen & Conzelmann 2014); or which ﬁrms stand to beneﬁt from the internationalization of standard- setting (DeSombre 2000; Büthe & Mattli 2013).3 However, few studies have inquired into the conditions under which rule-setting transnational governance arrangements are most likely to be procedurally rigorous and create standards with the potential to achieve desirable regulatory outcomes.4 In the following section, I develop a case for using best practice compliance as a means of comparing ELOs across sectors and gauging the sincerity and credibility of their governance efforts. 3. Best practices: What are they, who deﬁnes them, and why are they important? Best practice guidelines for eco-labeling cover practices with general relevance, such as setting clear and measurable environmental objectives, including all relevant stakeholders in standard development, and subjecting rules and policies to public scrutiny. This makes them a useful tool for cross-sectoral comparison. While recommendations in best practice guidelines refer to broad operating principles and not standard content, I argue that compliance with best practice is, nonetheless, a good proxy for the overall credibility of an ELO’s regulatory efforts. A high level of compliance demands signiﬁcant costs and behavioral changes on the part of the ELO and makes it very difﬁcult to develop meaningless standards. While best practice compliance is not a perfect proxy for rigorous standard content (a point I return to), there are a number of good reasons to believe that it can help separate credible governance efforts from superﬁcial ones. First, compliance involves relinquishing a considerable amount of control over the content of an eco-labeling standard. ELOs that are best practice compliant allow balanced groups of stakeholders to participate in standard development and decisionmaking, they cede control over certiﬁcation to third party entities, and they are fully independent from their funding sources. These factors make it nearly impossible for an ELO in full compliance with best practice to create a standard that serves only a narrow constituency or completely ignores environmental end-goals. Second, best practice compliance exposes ELOs to a higher than normal degree of public scrutiny. Compliant ELOs must be fully transparent across their operations. Many standard-setting bodies, particularly those seeking to advance a self-interested agenda, are simply unwilling to submit to this level of public scrutiny and, therefore, forgo voluntary best practice compliance. Third, best practice compliance involves hard costs. Compliant ELOs incur signiﬁcant expenses in seeking the input of marginalized stakeholders, monitoring their impacts, and publicly documenting their actions. Moreover, following best practices for stakeholder engagement can prolong the time needed to launch a new standard, leading to lost certiﬁcation revenues and providing opportunities for competing standards to gain a foothold in the market (Carmin et al. 2003, p. 529). For these reasons, best practice compliance is a good proxy for the likelihood an ELO will craft rigorous eco-labeling standards, and, thus, forms the dependent variable for this study. Best practice guidelines are developed by international organizations (the World Trade Organization [WTO], the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations [UN FAO]), associations of eco-labeling organizations (the International Social and Environmental Accreditation and Labeling [ISEAL] Alliance) and international standard-setting bodies (the International Organi- zation for Standardization [ISO]). They cover how ELOs should conduct their operations in the realms of standard setting, compliance assurance, and impact evaluation. Over the past 20 years, 10 guidelines on best practice in eco-labeling have been published by various organizations.5 While the recommendations contained in these guide- lines differ in many respects, there are also many areas of overlap. To create a singular measure of my dependent variable, I construct a unique Index of Best Practice (IBP) comprising 38 widely agreed upon recommendations of best practice drawn from the 10 existing guidelines for eco-labeling.5 The IBP was developed over four months and involved primary document analysis and key informant interviews with eco-labeling experts. I began by reviewing existing guidelines and identifying practices suggested by at least two different organizations (i.e. ISEAL and ISO). I then conducted interviews with six eco-labeling experts (including consultants, auditors, staff of accreditation bodies, and policymakers) to gain a better idea of which practices are most relevant to building and maintaining rigorous and credible standards. My objective throughout was to avoid biasing the IBP toward any one organization’s deﬁnition of best practice and to narrow the IBP down Best practice in eco-labeling H. van der Ven © 2015 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 278 Page 4: to the most relevant practices. The ﬁnal IBP comprises 38 recommendations for best practice in eco-labeling, grouped into 10 thematic categories and scored on the basis of full, partial, or non-compliance. These thematic categories, which mirror the ISEAL Credibility Principles (ISEAL Alliance 2013), and a sample of the recommen- dations contained in each category, are presented in Table 1. Details on how compliance is scored are provided in section 5. While a focus on best practice compliance is a useful tool for comparing ELOs across sectors, it is not a perfect proxy for rigor and credibility in eco-labeling. Thus, a few caveats should be applied at the outset of this study. First, while a focus on best practice can help us separate genuine from immaterial environmental claims, it cannot tell us whether a particular class of product is sustainable (i.e. palm oil). None of the existing best practice guidelines preclude the certiﬁcation of certain types of products or services that may be inherently harmful to the environment. Second, it should be recognized that the organizations that create best practices might have political motivations (Loconto & Fouilleux 2014). While it may be tempting to view such guidelines as benign exercises in regulatory optimization, in fact, they advance unique agendas ranging from global trade liberalization to the normalization of a market-driven and procedural vision of sustainability (Bernstein 2011; Loconto & Fouilleux 2014; Bernstein & van der Ven 2015). Third, best practices must demonstrate ﬁtness within a broader macro-political context (Mattli & Büthe 2005 p. 406), particularly the prevailing ideological consensus that barriers to free trade should be avoided at all cost. Fourth, and most importantly, best practices tell us little about the actual content of eco-labeling standards. It is entirely possible that two ELOs that are equally compliant with best practice could create very different standards, one much less rigorous than the other. While all of these concerns are valid, there remain good reasons to focus on compliance with best practice. In addition to the reasons offered earlier in this section, best practice compliance prevents some of the worst abuses of regulatory power. For example, it is more difﬁcult for an ELO to create a fraudulent eco-label if it has an organi- zational commitment to transparency. Similarly, the risk of regulatory capture is lessened if an ELO has policies in place to prevent the dominance of a single interest group. Thus, while compliance with best practice does not guarantee rigorous standards, it certainly increases the likelihood that an eco-label will be more than a hollow exercise in greenwashing. Second, concerns about the political motives of best practice guidelines are moderated by the procedures through which the IBP and individual guidelines are constructed.6 As noted above, the IBP is an amalgam of multiple best practice guidelines, each of which emphasizes different aspects of best practice. Hence, the conception of best practice presented in this study does not represent any organization’s unitary or politicized vision. Moreover, each of the guidelines that feed into the IBP is constructed through a rigorous, multi-stakeholder, consensus-based process. Hence, the potential for any one interest group to inﬂuence the content of best practice guidelines is mitigated by checks and balances designed to prevent the overt politicization of the process. In sum, while there remains a risk in focusing on best practice compliance, this risk is sufﬁciently mitigated to warrant its use as a broad measure of regulatory rigor and credibility. 4. Existing theories of rule-setting transnational governance In this section, I review previous attempts to theorize the conditions necessary for rigorous and credible transna- tional governance and specify the hypotheses to be tested in this study. In broad terms, past theories tend to focus on one of the following questions: (i) who is involved in the governance scheme; (ii) where does the governance scheme take place; and (iii) which sector or issue area does the governance scheme cover? 4.1. Who is involved in the governance scheme? One of the most widely used means of determining whether a transnational governance scheme will be rigorous and credible is by examining who is involved. A key axis of variation in this category concerns an ELO’s relationship to the state, which is often simpliﬁed through labels such as“public”and“private.”I deﬁne public ELOs as those directly connected to the institutions of the state; this includes organizations established by subunits of government, supranational entities, or units of intergovernmental organizations acting quasi-independently of national decisions (Eberlein et al. 2014, p. 3). Conversely, private ELOs are not owned, governed, or primarily funded by states. This category includes industry-funded organizations, environmental non-governmental organization (ENGO) initia- tives, and freestanding enterprises with neither ENGO nor industry involvement. A number of existing theories point to the inherent weakness of private organizations in crafting credible transnational governance schemes. Critics argue that private governance schemes are generally less representative than their public counterparts (Carmin et al. 2003). The “rules” of private governance schemes are rarely, if ever, created with the consent and participation of the subjects of regulation (Büthe 2010b, p. 19; Fuchs & Kalfagianni 2010, p. 10). This can have important consequences for the rigor of a governance initiative because it curtails proper feedback mechanisms. Additionally, skeptics suggest that accountability in private governance schemes is generally lacking (Van Harten 2005, p. 615; Fuchs & Kalfagianni 2010, p. 10). This lack of accountability can lead to problems in the compliance and enforcement of eco-labeling rules, and, subsequently, diminished regulatory rigor. Furthermore, a number of scholars have expressed concern about the impartiality of private forms of transna- tional governance. These concerns center mainly on industry-sponsored transnational governance initiatives. Mattli and Büthe (2005) suggest agents that are ﬁnancially dependent on a principal will have strong incentives to take the interests of their funders into account at the expense of other interests (p. 405). Neo-Gramscian scholars suggest that private forms of governance exist principally to stabilize the hegemonic capitalist worldview and reproduce a corporate-friendly global governance system (Levy & Newell 2002, p. 84; Schäferhoff et al. 2009, p. 455). Viewed from these perspectives, the primary objective of industry-sponsored ELOs (i.e. the Sustainable Forestry Initiative) is not environmental, rather it is to demonstrate just enough regulatory effort to mitigate anxiety about the environmental impacts of certain industries. If these assumptions are true, one might expect to see less attention to best practices in industry-sponsored ELOs relative to non-industry sponsored ELOs. This lack of independence may also be evident in for-proﬁt certiﬁcation and labeling bodies. Such organizations leave themselves vulnerable to downward pressure because their prosperity depends on expanding their clientele. In keeping with the notion that standards are much more likely to be adopted when the costs of compliance are kept low (Cashore et al. 2007, p. 163; Mayer & Gerefﬁ2010, p. 14), it could be argued that for-proﬁt ELOs have a structural imperative to ignore best practices that would raise the cost of compliance for potential clients. The following hypotheses ﬂow from this discussion: H1: Private ELOs will be less likely to meet best practice than public ELOs. H2: ELOs receiving more than 50 percent of their initial funding from a corporation or industry association will be less likely to meet best practice than non-industry-funded ELOs. H3: ELOs legally registered as not-for-proﬁt organizations will be more likely to meet best practice than for-proﬁt ELOs. Best practice in eco-labeling H. van der Ven © 2015 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 280 Page 6: Outside of theories that focus on the public–private relationship, a number of authors have previously found NGO inﬂuence to be a relevant factor in ensuring the rigor and credibility of a governance scheme (i.e. Corell & Betsill 2001; Gulbrandsen & Andresen 2004; Gulbrandsen 2010). ENGO participation can act as a voice for public concerns and provide a check on industry inﬂuence (Büthe & Mattli 2013, p. 220). In such situations, we may expect deep ENGO involvement in an ELO to lead to increased attention to best practice out of a desire to serve public ends, maintain ENGO credibility, and balance interests within a governance body. H4: ELOs that were originally funded or initiated by a preexisting environmental NGO will be more likely to meet best practice than ELOs that were not funded or initiated by a preexisting environmental NGO. 4.2. Where does the governance scheme take place? Another category of theories focuses on the physical location of transnational governance bodies and the unique impact of domestic institutional context on ELOs. A useful distinction can be made on the variety of capitalism (VOC) that characterizes the country in which an ELO’s headquarters is located. Two types of institutional contexts are commonly identiﬁed in VOC literature: Liberal Market Economies (LMEs) and Coordinated Market Economies (CMEs) (Hall & Soskice 2001, p. vi).7 Whereas LMEs tend to have decentralized systems where certiﬁcation schemes are run like for-proﬁt institutions and expected to compete for customers (Tate 2001, p. 446), CMEs often have supportive institutions which seek to encourage collaboration between standard-setters and provide resources to support shared innovation (Tate 2001, p. 446). Examples of such institutions include the Dutch Sustainable Trade Initiative (IDH), the German Gesellschaft für Internationale Zusammenarbeit (GIZ), and the Japan Environmental Management Association for Industry (JEMAI). The CME/LME distinction is a parsimonious way of determining whether the presence of state-funded supportive institutions leads to shared learning and innovation around best practices. If such institutions do facilitate learning and innovation, we might expect ELOs headquartered in CME countries to be more compliant with best practice than their counterparts in LMEs or other countries: H5: ELOs headquartered in CME countries will be more likely to meet best practice than ELOs headquartered in non-CME countries. Physical location can also be important for learning and diffusion processes. Previous research has examined the effect of speciﬁc territorial dynamics on business innovation (Bathelt et al. 2011, p. 3). If location-speciﬁc attributes and existing institutions can affect innovation in the business world, then it is plausible that they may equally affect innovation in transnational governance. Spatial proximity facilitates regular face-to-face meetings and conventions, which can stimulate further learning and knowledge generation (Bathelt et al. 2011, p. 3). These same processes may drive groups of ELOs clustered within the same city to collaborate and push each other toward more stringent compliance with best practice: H6: ELOs headquartered in cities where there is a high concentration of other ELOs will be more likely to meet best practice than ELOs headquartered in cities with a low concentration of other ELOs. 4.3. Which sector or issue area does the governance scheme cover? The ﬁnal subset of theories focuses on sector and issue-speciﬁc dynamics that may affect the rigor of governance activities. One notable group of such theories focuses on the role of competition. Competition between ELOs operating within a particular sector can often serve to drive regulatory rigor downward as ELOs aim to expand the number of entities certiﬁed to their standard. Competing ELOs lower compliance criteria in a bid to attract self-interested ﬁrms “shopping” for the most cost-effective certiﬁcation scheme amidst a buffet of choices (Gulbrandsen 2005; Abbott & Snidal 2010, p. 324). The resulting dynamic has been characterized as a regulatory “race-to-the-bottom” (Fransen 2011, p. 359). Other scholars in this ﬁeld have posited the reverse argument, suggesting a “California effect” whereby competition ratchets-up the rigor of standards (Vogel 1995; Overdevest 2010; Overdevest & Zeitlin 2014). However, because empirical evidence of downward pressure has been well documented amongst competing forestry eco-labels (Gulbrandsen 2005, p. 349), the race-to-the-bottom dynamic is more likely. While the cross-sectional data presented cannot show change in best practice compliance over time, a signiﬁcant correlation between ELOs who operate in highly competitive sectors and disregard for best practice would suggest the plausibility of the following hypothesis: Best practice in eco-labeling H. van der Ven © 2015 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 281 Page 7: H7: ELOs in direct competition with multiple competing standards in the same sector, practice, or issue area will be less likely to meet best practice than ELOs in non-competitive sectors or issue areas. 5. Statistical analysis: Data and methods I test the hypotheses outlined in the preceding section using an original dataset of 123 ELOs comprising, to the best extent possible, the full population of transnational ELOs in 2013. This is the ﬁrst dataset to combine information on best practice compliance in eco-labeling alongside data on organizational, contextual, and sector-speciﬁc attri- butes across a large number of cases. The dataset was built over ﬁve months from August to December 2013 and represents a cross-sectional snapshot of ELO policies and practices during this period. I draw data from three sources. First, I draw upon public documents from ELO websites. Second, I use the online Ecolabel Index (2015) database to identify the population of cases that meet my scope conditions and to locate ELO headquarters.8 Third, I draw upon email exchanges with ELO employees in situations where data was missing from public documents. The principal investigator performed all coding, but another researcher, using only the codebook provided, indepen- dently coded a sample of observations. Results between the principal investigator and the independent researcher were then compared to ensure commensurability. Individual ELOs, the owners of eco-labeling standards, are the unit of analysis in this study. In selecting cases for inclusion in the dataset, I apply a number of scope conditions to ensure comparability. First, ELOs included in the dataset must be transnational in presence; meaning that their eco-labels are present in more than one country and that certiﬁcation to their standards is available to ﬁrms headquartered in a different country than the ELO. Second, ELOs must develop at least one standard/eco-label that is primarily environmental in its objectives. ELOs operating standards that include environmental objectives only as a secondary or tertiary consideration (i.e. Fairtrade) are excluded from this analysis. Third, ELOs must have at least one environmental standard/eco-label that is currently active. Fourth, ELOs must have at least one eco-label that is consumer facing and cannot exclusively develop business-to-business (B2B) standards. Fifth, ELOs cannot solely develop in-house standards/eco-labels (i.e. Nike Considered Design). Sixth, ELOs must develop standards that denote achievement of a speciﬁc objective, not merely support for a broad set of principles (i.e. UN Global Compact). Finally, ELOs included in the dataset cannot solely develop standards that apply to a natural area or a professional designation. The dependent variable (DV) for this study is the IBP discussed in section 3 of this paper. Each ELO in my dataset is evaluated against the IBP using a simple scoring system. On each of the 38 recommendations in the IBP, an ELO can score zero for non-compliance, one for partial compliance, or two for full compliance. I calculate an ELO’s score across each relevant IBP recommendation and then sum the total compliance score.9 I then divide the total compliance score by the maximum possible score to arrive at a number between one and zero (see Equation 1). I use a proportion in lieu of a simple count because certain recommendations in the IBP are not universally applicable to all ELOs in the dataset. An ELO that is fully compliant with best practice would score a one on the IBP, whereas one that disregards them entirely would score a zero. A summary of the IVs, their afﬁliated hypotheses, and coding rules is presented in Table 2. Readers should note that the dichotomous variables in the “who governs” category are not mutually exclusive. It is possible for an ELO to be coded as both industry-funded and initiated by an existing ENGO (as is the case for the Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil). In addition to the IVs, I also control for the effects of a number of variables that lack an extensive theoretical foundation in the literature, but hold common sense explanatory value. First, I control for whether an ELO explicitly develops standards for either food or ﬁsheries because the reputational stakes for ﬁrms operating in these sectors tend to be higher and, therefore, may exert upward pressure on best practice compliance (Mayer & Gerefﬁ2010, Best practice in eco-labeling H. van der Ven © 2015 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 282 Page 8: p. 9; Fransen & Conzelmann 2014, p. 4). Second, I control for the year an ELO launched its ﬁrst eco-labeling standard. This control is meant to highlight any temporal dynamics affecting compliance with best practice.10 Third, I control for the number of standards, both environmental and otherwise, that an ELO currently manages and the number of countries in which products bearing an ELO’s most widely used standard can currently be purchased. Both of these variables are proxy measures for organizational size and capacity. Fourth, I control for whether an ELO develops standards across more than one commercial/industrial sector and whether an ELO’s most widely used standard examines multiple environmental attributes in determining whether a product/service can be certiﬁed. Finally, I control for gross domestic product per capita in an ELO’s headquarter country. This variable is a proxy for a country’s overall wealth and helps separate the effect of afﬂuence on best practice compliance from the effect of supportive institutions that may be present in CME countries. To accommodate the bounded nature of my DV, I follow Baum’s (2008) suggestion to use a logit transformation (p. 299). This allows me to model y* (the logit transformation of IBP score) as a linear function of a set of regressors (X). The operation is summarized in Equation 2. Having transformed my DV to allow for linear modeling, I then use ordinary least squares (OLS) regression analysis.
